summary: This cluster focuses on the restoration and transplantation of the brown alga *Ecklonia cava* in coastal environments, specifically investigating optimal methods to counteract habitat degradation and declining seaweed biodiversity. Research centered on the successful transplantation of *Ecklonia cava* sporophytes onto artificial seaweed reef structures, with key findings demonstrating that smaller blades (5-10 cm) transplanted in March exhibited significantly higher survival and growth rates compared to larger blades (20-30 cm) transplanted later in April. The studies collectively emphasize that precise blade size and seasonal timing are critical parameters for the effective re-establishment of this species in degraded coastal ecosystems.
